title: Collection model for the 2023 Census | Stats NZ
content:
## Collection model for the 2023 Census
28 September 2022, 1:00pm
Collection model for the 2023 Census describes the field design of the 2023 Census and how everyone in Aotearoa New Zealand will be enabled to participate in the census.
The 2023 Census is focused on achieving high response rates. This will help us produce census data that represents both Aotearoa New Zealand as a whole and local communities and will provide quality data that ensures our country can to provide the services people need .
The 2023 Census will be run between 13 February and April 2023, with Census Day scheduled to take place in the middle of this period on Tuesday, 7 March 2023.
We are doing some things differently in 2023 to make it easier for people to be counted in the census – including providing more choice in how people complete their census forms and more help for those who need it.
- People will have the choice of completing their census forms either online or on paper. Approximately 44 percent of households will receive paper forms (as well as their online code) before Census Day, compared with 3 percent of households in 2018. Paper forms will be available on request in other areas.
- There will be more assistance for people to complete their census forms. There will be more census collectors across the motu – close to double the number of collectors that were available in 2018. We will deliver census materials earlier so people can participate in the census earlier. Households in communities with some of the highest numbers of non-response in 2018 will have their packs hand delivered by trained census collectors. All dwellings will have the chance to receive help to take part in the census, as needed. We will make help available in communities, including organising events where anyone can ask questions about the census and how to participate.
- There will be more census collectors across the motu – close to double the number of collectors that were available in 2018.
- We will deliver census materials earlier so people can participate in the census earlier.
- Households in communities with some of the highest numbers of non-response in 2018 will have their packs hand delivered by trained census collectors.
- All dwellings will have the chance to receive help to take part in the census, as needed.
- We will make help available in communities, including organising events where anyone can ask questions about the census and how to participate.
- We are adding a pre-Census Day reminder letter to prompt self-response earlier than in previous censuses.
- After Census Day, we will start follow-up visits earlier to get forms from and offer help to non-responders as quickly as possible.
- We will be recruiting census collectors from within the communities they will work in, as locals are best placed to communicate with and encourage other locals to participate in the census.

## 2023 Census field design
Dwellings (where people live or stay) are separated into the three ‘streams’ of:
The three streams allow us to tailor our collections approach to individual dwellings and communities. Our more resource-intensive Assist and Deliver approaches provide face-to-face contact, assistance, dedicated communications, and community-driven activities.
A dwelling will be allocated a particular stream based on a range of factors. These include geographical area, mailability and internet access, demographic variables in the dwelling’s community, and whether historically there has been non- or low response in the local community.
We are directing more resources to geographic areas with a higher proportion of groups that have historically been underrepresented in census data. In particular, we are using our Assist and Deliver streams to focus on raising response rates for Māori, Pacific peoples, and other populations with historically lower response rates.
2023 Census Collection Model (PDF)
- The Assist stream will be delivered to approximately 15 percent of dwellings.
- The Assist stream is our most resource-intensive approach. It involves using face-to-face contact to increase response rates in priority response groups.
- There are a minimum of three visits to attempt contact and deliver census forms in person, with census collectors encouraging participation and offering people support to complete census forms.
- Forms will be left at the dwelling on the third visit if contact has still not been made.
- Census collectors will visit dwellings that have not completed and returned their census forms during the follow-up phase after Census Day. If there is no one home when they visit, the collectors will leave extra census form packs and notes reminding the household to complete their forms.
- The Deliver stream will be provided to approximately 15 percent of dwellings.
- Like the Assist stream, census forms will be hand delivered to dwellings in the Deliver stream, with face-to-face contact attempted.
- One attempt at contact will be made to hand deliver paper forms, verify the address, and check for extra dwellings and the number of occupants.
- Forms will be left at the dwelling if contact has not been made.
- During the follow-up phase, census collectors will visit dwellings in this stream that have not completed and returned all or any of their forms. This visit will happen within nine days of Census Day. All mailable addresses will also receive reminder letters during this follow-up phase.
- The Mailout stream represents approximately 70 percent of all dwellings.
- Dwellings are mailed either a letter with an internet code to complete their census online or a paper forms pack to complete and return via freepost.
- Those provided with a paper forms pack can, and are encouraged to, complete their census online.
- During the follow-up phase, any dwellings in the Mailout stream that have not completed and returned their census will receive a mix of census collector visits and mailed reminders.
- For the 2023 Census, 20 percent of dwellings in the Mailout stream will receive paper packs (meaning 44 percent of all dwellings will receive paper forms). This includes areas where there were either high paper-response or high non-response rates in the 2018 Census; a high proportion of usual older residents, usual Māori residents, and usual Pacific residents; and dwellings with no digital capability. (Updated 16 January 2023)
- We identified 3 percent of areas throughout the Mailout stream that are near the boundary line of whether they should be sent paper packs or online access codes. Half of these areas were randomly selected to receive paper packs. We will compare the results of the areas that receive paper packs versus those that receive internet access codes. This comparison will show the effect of initially receiving paper packs on outcomes such as response rate, whether response was online or on paper, response time, number of census collector visits needed during follow-up, and quality of data. What we learn will help us target where to send paper packs in the future. (Updated 16 January 2023)

## Pre-Census Day reminder
For the first time, the 2023 Census will feature the addition of a pre-Census Day reminder t o shift forward as much self-response as possible.
The pre-Census Day reminder is a letter to all mailable dwellings in the Mailout and Deliver streams (approximately 77 percent of dwellings) during the week before Census Day.
The 2022 field test for the 2023 Census demonstrated that a pre-Census Day reminder, followed by the non-response follow-up (NRFU) approach, had a positive effect on dwelling response rates.
Using the reminder to bring forward self-response will help reduce the number of field staff required during the follow-up phase by approximately 120 full time equivalent ( FTE) staff, and it means that the follow-up can be more effectively focused on those who still fail to respond.
The collection model for the 2023 Census includes plans to have census collectors visit all non-responding and partially responding dwellings. This will be done earlier than in 2018.
There will also be more follow-up visits than in 2018 and a focus on face-to-face contact for getting responses from priority populations.
The people we employ and train to collect the census are called census collectors. For the 2023 Census, we will recruit the census collectors locally to reflect the communities they will work in.
We have significantly increased the amount of field effort and availability of staff to support respondents. There will be around double the census collector resource in 2023 compared with 2018, with additional census collectors in all areas.
The finalised field staff numbers for the 2023 Census are:
- 3,534 field staff, with a 70 percent full-time / 30 percent part-time split.
The breakdown of staff numbers by position is outlined in the table below.
Operational Leadership Staff (head count, FTE)
Operational Leadership Staff (head count, FTE)
National Operations Manager
National Operations Manager
Staffing levels will vary across geographic areas, with higher numbers in the Far North, Kaipara, Whangārei, Ōpōtiki, East Cape, Gisborne, Rotorua, South Auckland, and some other smaller North Island areas to allow for more contact and provision of help.
